frameworks.name: hidden Markov model
frameworks.one_line: Latent discrete states segment trial-by-trial choice behavior

frameworks.name: single-strategy psychometric model
frameworks.one_line: Classical view assuming one consistent decision rule per session

frameworks.name: lapse-rate model
frameworks.one_line: Reframes attributed lapses as transitions into biased states

frameworks.name: perceptual decision-making
frameworks.one_line: Sensory-evidence-driven choice as the behavioral domain

core_question: Do subjects use a single consistent decision strategy, or alternate between discrete strategies within a session?
key_assumptions: Choice strategies can be captured as discrete latent states
State transitions are Markovian across trials
Strategy states are shared structure across individuals
manipulations: Fit hidden Markov models to mouse and human choice data
Compared multi-state HMM against single-strategy baselines
key_findings: Choice behavior reflects multiple interleaved strategies rather than one
Identified states persist tens to hundreds of trials before switching
Mouse strategies were highly consistent across individuals
One 'engaged' stimulus-driven state plus several biased error-prone states
interpretations: Apparent lapses reflect transitions into biased strategy states, not noise
Standard performance measures mask dramatic within-session strategy changes
